title: San Rafael Police Department Human Trafficking

communications.subject: California Public Records Act Request: San Rafael Police Department Human Trafficking
communications.communication:
To Whom It May Concern:

Pursuant to the California Public Records Act, I hereby request the following records:

All documents produced between January 1st, 2023 until March 16th 2026 that include the term "human trafficking" "Eighteenth Street Gang" "Nortenos" "Mafia" "Nuestra Familia" "homeless" "unhoused" produced by the San Rafael police department. 

All police reports pertaining to human trafficking produced between January 1st, 2023 until March 16th 2026.

The requested documents will be made available to the general public, and this request is not being made for commercial purposes.

In the event that there are fees, I would be grateful if you would inform me of the total charges in advance of fulfilling my request. I would prefer the request filled electronically, by e-mail attachment if available or CD-ROM if not.

Thank you in advance for your anticipated cooperation in this matter. I look forward to receiving your response to this request within 10 calendar days, as the statute requires.

Sincerely,

Robbie Powelson

title: Request for Public Records Request Logs BPHC

communications.subject: Massachusetts Public Records Law Request: Request for Public Records Request Logs BPHC
communications.communication:
To Whom It May Concern:

Pursuant to the Massachusetts Public Records Law, I hereby request the following records:

I request a copy of any logs, spreadsheets, or databases maintained by your office that track public records requests received, including (if available):

Date of request
Requester name
Summary or description of request
Response date
Disposition (granted, denied, etc.)
Fee charged, if any

If available, please provide the data in spreadsheet or machine-readable format (e.g., CSV or Excel).

Please provide 2024 and 2025.

I also request that, if appropriate, fees be waived as we believe this request is in the public interest, as suggested but not stipulated by the recommendations of the Massachusetts Supervisor of Public Records. The requested documents will be made available to the general public free of charge as part of the public information service at MuckRock.com, and is not made for commercial usage.

I expect the request to be filled in an accessible format, including for screen readers, which provide text-to-speech for persons unable to read print. Files that are not accessible to screen readers include, for example, .pdf image files as well as physical documents.

In the event that there are fees, I would be grateful if you would inform me of the total charges in advance of fulfilling my request. I would prefer the request filled electronically, by e-mail attachment if available or CD-ROM if not.

Thank you in advance for your anticipated cooperation in this matter. I look forward to receiving your response to this request within 10 business days, as the statute requires.

Sincerely,

Brian McCarter

communications.subject: RE: Massachusetts Public Records Law Request: Request for Public Records Request Logs BPHC

To Whom It May Concern:

I wanted to follow up on the following Massachusetts Public Records Law request, copied below, and originally submitted on July 27, 2025. Please let me know when I can expect to receive a response.

Thanks for your help, and let me know if further clarification is needed.

communications.subject: Fee Estimate RE: Massachusetts Public Records Law Request: Request for Public Records Request Logs BPHC
communications.communication:
Dear Mr. McCarter,

The Boston Public Health Commission (“BPHC”) is in receipt of your request for public records regarding the above-referenced matter; this request was received by our office on June 28, 2025.

Description of work to be done

To fully comply with your request, an extensive search / review will need to be conducted of the responsive records. As part of this review, we will need to segregate and redact any records to the extent they contain information exempted under the Massachusetts Public Records Law. A preliminary search found that BPHC’s Public Record Request Spreadsheet has a total of 133 public records requests from 2024 and 2025. We estimate that it will take BPHC personnel approximately 19 hours to complete our review / redaction and provide the responsive records.

Please note, as you will soon see, BPHC filed a fee petition seeking permission to charge for segregating and redacting records to the extent they contain information exempted under state and federal law. We currently estimate that the 133 public record requests will need to be reviewed for segregation and redaction at approximately 7 minutes per entries. Once the records are reproduced in final form, they can be transmitted to you. Please see below for the estimated time to complete the work described below:

  *   Review: 2.00 hours
  *   Segregation and redaction time: 16 hours
  *   Reproduction time: 1.00 hour
  *   Total estimated time: 19 hours

Hourly rate and estimated fee
Hourly rate: $25.00/hour. This hourly rate is equal to or less than the hourly rate attributed to the lowest paid employee who has the necessary skill required to search for, compile, segregate, redact or reproduce the records requested.

No fee will be assessed for the first two hours of work. Therefore, the estimated cost of complying with your request is 17 hours of chargeable work at $25.00 per hour, or $425.00. This amount must be paid in advance before production of responsive records. See G. L. c. 66, § 10(a)(iii).

Upon payment of this amount ($425.00), BPHC will review and release such records that are not exempted under Public Records Law. If you wish for BPHC to proceed in review of these records, kindly remit payment by check or money order payable to the Boston Public Health Commission. Mail payment to the Office of the General Counsel, Boston Public Health Commission, 1010 Massachusetts Avenue, 6th floor, Boston MA 02118.

Sincerely,
Javier

Javier R. Salas
Assistant General Counsel
Boston Public Health Commission
1010 Massachusetts Avenue, 6th Floor
Boston, MA 02118
Phone: (617) 534-7158
jsalas@bphc.org<mailto:jsalas@bphc.org>

communications.subject: RE: Massachusetts Public Records Law Request: Request for Public Records Request Logs BPHC
communications.communication:
Dear Supervisor of Records,

I respectfully object to the Boston Public Health Commission's (BPHC) July 2025 fee petition SPR25-2312 for 19 hours of review time to produce its public records request log. This fee is legally unjustified, factually unsupported, and violates the spirit and letter of the Massachusetts Public Records Law. The petition should be denied in full for the reasons below:

1. Request logs are simple, standard records maintained electronically in spreadsheet or database format. They are not complex records requiring line-by-line review.

2. The requested log is presumptively public and has been routinely ordered produced by your office in many past cases. It is a foundational transparency document.

3. Any redactions required under HIPAA or Exemption (c) would already have been applied when the underlying requests were processed for other requesters. If personal health information was present, it would have been redacted or withheld then. BPHC cannot double-bill for previously completed work.

4. HIPAA and Exemption (c) do not cover administrative summaries of records requests. The fee petition never asserts that log entries include medical records or identifying health details nor would that be typical. BPHC offers no basis to claim every entry poses a HIPAA issue, and certainly no specific justification as to which entries do.

5. The fee petition fails the "actually necessary" test under 950 CMR 32.06(4)(g). No detailed breakdown is provided for the claimed 7 minutes of review per entry. BPHC offers only speculative assertions that every one of the 133 log entries must be reviewed individually.

6. The Public Records Law imposes a duty to facilitate access (G.L. c. 66, § 10(a); 950 CMR 32.07(1)(b), (d), (f)). If BPHC's log is structured in a way that makes it difficult to segregate or export, that is a failure of their own recordkeeping system, not a burden that should be shifted to the requester.

7. Conditioning production on payment of inflated fees constitutes constructive denial. This delays access in violation of 950 CMR 32.06(2)(i) and G.L. c. 66, § 10(a).

8. As a matter of electronic record management, the log should be produced in its native spreadsheet format under 950 CMR 32.07(1)(c) and (f). Copying and exporting require negligible time.

9. The Supervisor has repeatedly rejected similar boilerplate redaction burdens in prior cases. In SPR18/1234, the MBTA claimed it could not easily export internal affairs log data, but the Supervisor found this unconvincing and ordered the records released efficiently and without excessive fees. That case mirrors the structure of BPHC’s log and reinforces the requirement that agencies must use technology to reduce costs, not inflate them.

10. The public interest in this record is well established. I was recently cited in The Boston Guardian (Vol. 10, Issue 8) for my work on public records and government transparency. The article highlighted my use of public records law to investigate BPHC's role in the Mass and Cass crisis, including failures of enforcement, data disclosure, and accountability. A second article in the same issue focused squarely on the public safety breakdown in the South End and cited Mass and Cass as an epicenter of drug use, violence, and open-air crime. It reported the growing public pressure for more oversight of harm reduction programs under BPHC’s control. The public records request log is directly relevant to this debate, as it would show how BPHC has responded to transparency demands over time  including patterns of delay, denial, or cooperation. These records are critical for policymakers, journalists, and community leaders seeking to understand whether BPHC’s internal posture aligns with its public messaging. This context plainly supports a full fee waiver under 950 CMR 32.07(2)(k).

11. This fits a broader pattern by BPHC of using overbroad HIPAA claims and inflated fee estimates to chill access. 

12. If the agency previously denied other requests on HIPAA or other exemption grounds, then there are no responsive records to list in the log. There would be nothing to redact. BPHC cannot claim that records are both denied and somehow still require full HIPAA redaction in the log.

13. Agencies have superior knowledge of their own systems and must use that knowledge to retrieve records efficiently. If BPHC cannot filter its log quickly, it reflects poor records management and does not justify a fee.

14. The log is functionally analogous to a police incident log, and BPHC maintains its own law enforcement personnel. While G.L. c. 41, § 98F explicitly applies to police logs, the underlying principle — that public accountability logs must be freely accessible — applies by analogy here. BPHC cannot evade this obligation by labeling its own oversight records as exempt.

15. Nothing in the request would lead to the appearance of private health data. The request simply asked for a log of prior public records requests a record type that agencies across the Commonwealth routinely provide. There is no indication that any entry references private treatment, diagnosis, or care, and BPHC never argues otherwise.

16. Requestor names are not categorically exempt. Public records request logs maintained by numerous municipalities and agencies — including Boston, Worcester, Brookline, and the Department of Environmental Protection — routinely release requester names without incident. There is no legal basis to suggest requester names are protected under HIPAA or Exemption (c) in this context.

17. BPHC admits the log exists in spreadsheet format, which undercuts the entire claimed burden. If any column consistently contains exempt data (which is doubtful), it could be removed in under 10 seconds using one left-click to select and one right-click to delete. This is basic spreadsheet functionality. The idea that 19 hours of review is required to handle a 133-row spreadsheet is facially absurd. The petition never explains why this task cannot be performed through standard filters, deletions, or exports -all of which are consistent with your office’s expectations for electronically maintained records.

Requested Relief:

Deny BPHC's fee petition in its entirety.
Order BPHC to produce the public records request log electronically.
Grant a full fee waiver under 950 CMR 32.07(2)(k), based on the public interest and non-commercial nature of the request.
If BPHC believes some entries are partially exempt, order them to produce redacted logs on a rolling basis and submit any withheld material for in camera review.

Thank you for your consideration.

Respectfully submitted,

Brian McCarter

communications.subject: RE: Massachusetts Public Records Law Request: Request for Public Records Request Logs BPHC
communications.communication:
Follow-Up Submission – SPR25/2312 (BPHC Fee Petition)
On August 8, 2025, BPHC filed a fee petition with the Supervisor (SPR25/2312) claiming 19 hours of review/segregation were “necessary” to fulfill my July 27, 2025 request for its public records request log. By definition under 950 CMR 32.06(4), this represents that the RAO had already determined the scope of responsive records and the actual work required to produce them.

On August 8, 2025, I filed my rebuttal objecting to the petition in full.

On August 11, 2025—three days after filing the petition,  BPHC issued a separate letter invoking a 15-business-day extension “for review and production” of the same records. This sequence undermines the credibility and sufficiency of the fee petition:

If BPHC truly had completed the review necessary to calculate a 19-hour burden on August 8, there would be no basis to claim, on August 11, that additional time was needed for review before production.

Conversely, if further review was needed, then the August 8 fee petition was speculative, not an “actually necessary” estimate under 950 CMR 32.06(4)(g).
This inconsistency reinforces the points already raised in my initial objection—particularly that the petition fails the “actually necessary” test and constitutes constructive denial by conditioning access on prepayment of an inflated, unsubstantiated fee.
I have attached the August 11, 2025 extension letter for the Supervisor’s review.

Respectfully,
Brian McCarter

Please forward to: Michael Manning, Esq., Office of the Secretary of the Commonwealth, Public Records Division

title: EPA Records re: Grove Bags / TerpLoc Enforcement

communications.subject: Freedom of Information Act Request: EPA Records re: Grove Bags / TerpLoc Enforcement
communications.communication:
To Whom It May Concern:

Pursuant to the Freedom of Information Act, I hereby request the following records:

This is a request under the Freedom of Information Act, 5 U.S.C. § 552.

I request copies of all records in the possession of the U.S. Environmental Protection Agency relating to Grove Bags (also known as Kinzie/Kenzie Advanced Polymers, d/b/a Grove Bags) and its product line TerpLoc® bags, from January 1, 2024 through the present.

Specifically, please include:

1. Referrals, notices, or communications received from the Washington State Department of Agriculture (WSDA) concerning Grove Bags or TerpLoc products.
2. Any communications between EPA and WSDA, or other state pesticide regulators, regarding Grove Bags/TerpLoc.
3. Internal EPA communications (emails, memoranda, notes) discussing Grove Bags/TerpLoc, including enforcement, registration, or labeling issues.
4. Any EPA investigation files, inspection reports, or compliance determinations concerning Grove Bags/TerpLoc.

Please provide responsive records in electronic format (PDFs or native email files if available). If portions are withheld, please state the statutory basis for each withholding and release all reasonably segregable portions.

To the extent possible, please expedite this request because the records involve ongoing enforcement actions and significant public interest.

Thank you for your assistance.

The requested documents will be made available to the general public, and this request is not being made for commercial purposes.

In the event that there are fees, I would be grateful if you would inform me of the total charges in advance of fulfilling my request. I would prefer the request filled electronically, by e-mail attachment if available or CD-ROM if not.

Thank you in advance for your anticipated cooperation in this matter. I look forward to receiving your response to this request within 20 business days, as the statute requires.

Sincerely,

cannanewsreports

communications.communication:
Dear cannanewsreports cannanewsreports, 

Thank you for submitting your Freedom of Information Act (FOIA) request. 
Case Number 2026-EPA-00078 has been assigned to this request. In all future correspondence regarding this request, please reference your case number. 
If you selected EPA National FOIA Office (for all non-OIG requests), your request was transmitted directly to the EPA’s National FOIA Office (NFO). NFO will confirm receipt and assignment of your request by sending you an Acknowledgement email within five business days. 
If you have any questions, please contact the FOIA Requester Service Center at hq.foia@epa.gov (mailto:hq.foia@epa.gov) or at 202-566-1667.
Regards, 
EPA National FOIA Office

Any communications between EPA and WSDA, or other state pesticide regulators, regarding Grove Bags/TerpLoc.3. Internal EPA communications (emails, memoranda, notes) discussing Grove Bags/TerpLoc, including enforcement, registration, or labeling issues.4. I would prefer the request filled electronically, by e-mail attachment if available or CD-ROM if not.Thank you in advance for your anticipated cooperation in this matter. I look forward to receiving your response to this request within 20 business days, as the statute requires.Sincerely,cannanewsreportsUpload documents directly: https://www.muckrock.com/respond/2100513/ (Date Range for Record Search: From 01/01/2024 To 10/03/2025)."Your FOIA request was received and assigned for processing. If you need any further assistance or would like to discuss any aspect of your request, you may seek assistance from EPA’s FOIA Public Liaison at hq.foia@epa.gov or call (202) 566-1667. You may also seek assistance from the Office of Government Information Services (OGIS). You may contact OGIS in any of the following ways: by mail, Office of Government Information Services, National Archives and Records Administration, 8601 Adelphi Road, College Park, MD 20740-6001; email: ogis@nara.gov; telephone: (202) 741-5770 or (877) 684-6448; or fax: (202) 741-5769.

National FOIA Office

title: California official reports RFP, agreements, correspondence (Supreme Court of California)

communications.subject: California Public Records Act Request: California official reports RFP, agreements, correspondence (Supreme Court of California)
communications.communication:
To Whom It May Concern:

Pursuant to the California Public Records Act, I hereby request the following records:

The below requests are all in connection with the California Supreme Court's Request for Proposal RFP-CAL-2023-01-RP for publication of the California official reports (the "RFP"), made under Government Code section 68903, and which is available at https://courts.ca.gov/request/rfp-cal-2023-01-rp/rfp/publication-california-official-reports.

Government Code section 68903 states:

"The official reports shall be published under a contract to be entered into on behalf of the state by the Chief Justice of California, the Secretary of State, the Attorney General, the President of the State Bar, and the Reporter of Decisions, who shall serve as secretary."

REQUESTS:

1. All signed agreements made under proposals received pursuant to the RFP, including subsequent signed amendments (collectively, the "Agreements").
2. All correspondence with LexisNexis about the RFP.
3. All correspondence with LexisNexis about the Agreements.
4. All proposals received in response to the RFP.
5. All internal correspondence related to the RFP.

If assessing any confidentiality concerns, please note the following language in the RFP:

11.0 CONFIDENTIAL OR PROPRIETARY INFORMATION
PROPOSALS ARE SUBJECT TO DISCLOSURE TO THIRD PARTIES AND MEMBERS OF THE PUBLIC PURSUANT TO APPLICABLE LAWS, INCLUDING PUBLIC DISCLOSURE PURSUANT TO RULE 10.500 OF THE CALIFORNIA RULES OF COURT. Except as required by law, neither the Judicial Council nor the Court will disclose (i) social security numbers, or (ii) balance sheets or income statements submitted by a Publisher that is not a publicly traded corporation. All other information in Proposals may be disclosed in response to applicable public records requests, or as otherwise required by law. Such disclosure may be made regardless of whether the Proposal (or portions thereof) is marked “confidential,” “proprietary,” “copyright ©,” or otherwise, and regardless of any statement in the Proposal (a) purporting to limit the Judicial Council’s or Court’s right to disclose information in the Proposal, or (b) requiring the Judicial Council or Court to inform or obtain the consent of the Publisher prior to the disclosure of the Proposal (or portions thereof). Any Proposal that is password protected, or contains portions that are password protected, may be rejected. Submission of any Proposal pursuant to this RFP constitutes acknowledgment and consent by the Publisher to the potential public disclosure of its Proposal content, as set forth in this section. Publishers are accordingly cautioned not to include confidential, proprietary, or privileged information in proposals.

The requested documents will be made available to the general public, and this request is not being made for commercial purposes.

In the event that there are fees, I would be grateful if you would inform me of the total charges in advance of fulfilling my request. I would prefer the request filled electronically, by e-mail attachment if available, or by USB drive, CD-ROM, or online file sharing system if not.

Thank you in advance for your anticipated cooperation in this matter. I look forward to receiving your response to this request within 10 calendar days, as the statute requires.

Sincerely,

Ansel Halliburton

communications.subject: Thank you for your request. (Autoreply)
communications.communication:
Thank you for contacting the Judicial Council of California’s Public Access to Judicial Administrative Records (PAJAR) team. This message is an automatic reply to let you know that we received your request.

Please read the message below because it has information that may help you.

*** PLEASE DO NOT REPLY to this e-mail message. Responses to this automatic reply are not monitored.***

WHAT TO EXPECT


  *   If you are asking for court case records—for example, a court order, divorce records, name change records, criminal history records— this message will be the only response you receive. Please read the section below, The PAJAR Team Cannot Provide These Records, to find information that may be helpful.


  *   If you are asking for judicial administrative records from the Judicial Council, the Supreme Court of California, or a California Court of Appeal, the PAJAR team will send you a response within about 10 days.


  *   Here are some examples of judicial administrative records: signed contracts, final audit reports, budget and spending reports, reports to the Legislature, and procurement records. You can find more information about judicial administrative records at courts.ca.gov/cms/rules/index/ten/rule10_500<https://courts.ca.gov/cms/rules/index/ten/rule10_500> and at courts.ca.gov/policy-administration/public-records<https://courts.ca.gov/policy-administration/public-records>.


  *   The PAJAR team may ask you for more information to be sure it understands your request. The PAJAR team may also notify you if it needs more time to complete a search.


  *   When work on your request is complete, the PAJAR team will notify you whether there are disclosable responsive records or not. If there are disclosable responsive copies, the PAJAR team will provide electronic copies, if possible.

THE PAJAR TEAM CANNOT PROVIDE THESE RECORDS

>   Court Case Records—Superior Courts of California

The PAJAR team cannot provide copies of superior court case records. If you want records filed in a specific superior court case, you must contact the court directly. California has 58 superior courts, one in each county. You can find links to each court’s website at courts.ca.gov/find-my-court<https://courts.ca.gov/find-my-court>. Each superior court clerk is responsible for that court’s records.

Some superior court websites allow the public to search and view some types of case records online. If a court’s website does not allow you to search and view case records online, you must call or visit the court clerk’s office for help. Superior courts post their telephone numbers and street addresses on their websites. Some also post e-mail addresses.

Examples of records that you must request by contacting a superior court:

·         Divorce decrees;

·         Traffic citations;

·         Name change records;

·         Wills;

·         Criminal case records (for example, orders imposing sentences and judgments); and

·         Restraining orders.

You will find more information about some of these topics in the message below.

>   Court Case Records—Supreme Court of California and California Courts of Appeal

The PAJAR team cannot provide copies of appellate court case records. You can find information about specific cases filed with the Supreme Court of California or with the California Courts of Appeal at appellatecases.courtinfo.ca.gov<http://appellatecases.courtinfo.ca.gov/>.

You can review opinions that the Supreme Court of California and the California Courts of Appeal issued in specific cases at courts.ca.gov/opinions.htm<http://www.courts.ca.gov/opinions.htm>.

You can find the following Supreme Court of California case records online:

·         Legal briefs filed in cases argued before the court: supreme.courts.ca.gov/case-information/briefs-argued-cases<https://supreme.courts.ca.gov/case-information/briefs-argued-cases>; and

·         Official minutes going back three years: supreme.courts.ca.gov/case-information/minutes<https://supreme.courts.ca.gov/case-information/minutes>.

To view or request copies of records filed in a specific case before the Supreme Court of California, you must call or visit the court. Supreme Court contact information is posted at supreme.courts.ca.gov/about-court/court-locations-and-contacts<https://supreme.courts.ca.gov/about-court/court-locations-and-contacts>.

To view or request copies of records filed in specific cases before a California Court of Appeal you must call or visit the court. You can find a list of the six Courts of Appeal at appellate.courts.ca.gov<https://appellate.courts.ca.gov/>. On the left side of that page, select the court where the case is filed. Then scroll down to select “Contact Us.” On that page you will find court telephone numbers and location addresses.

If you have a Court of Appeal case number, but you do not know the court where the case is filed, please visit appellate.courts.ca.gov<https://appellate.courts.ca.gov/>. That page lists the letters that begin the case numbers for each of the Courts of Appeal.

Information about California appellate court cases also is available at your local law library, which may provide free public access to computer-assisted legal research systems such as LexisNexis or Westlaw. To learn more about local public law libraries, and to locate one near you, visit selfhelp.courts.ca.gov/court-basics/law-libraries<https://selfhelp.courts.ca.gov/court-basics/law-libraries>.

>   Court Case Records—United States Federal Courts


The PAJAR team cannot provide copies of records filed in cases before federal courts located in California. To find information about those federal courts—including court telephone numbers and location addresses for those courts—please visit:

  *   www.cand.uscourts.gov<http://www.cand.uscourts.gov> (district court, Northern California);
  *   www.casd.uscourts.gov<http://www.casd.uscourts.gov> (district court, Southern California);
  *   www.caed.uscourts.gov<http://www.caed.uscourts.gov> (district court, Eastern California);
  *   www.cacd.uscourts.gov<http://www.cacd.uscourts.gov> (district court, Central California); and
  *   www.ca9.uscourts.gov<http://www.ca9.uscourts.gov> (Courts of Appeal).

>   Birth or Death Certificates

The PAJAR team cannot provide copies of birth or death certificates. The registrar or recorder in the county where the birth or death occurred would have these records.

You can find a list of all California county registrars and recorders, and their contact information, at www.cdph.ca.gov/Programs/CHSI/Pages/County-Registrars-and-Recorders.aspx<http://www.cdph.ca.gov/Programs/CHSI/Pages/County-Registrars-and-Recorders.aspx>.

For information about how to request a birth or death certificate, please visit the website of California’s Department of Public Health, at www.cdph.ca.gov/Programs/CHSI/Pages/Vital-Records.aspx<http://www.cdph.ca.gov/Programs/CHSI/Pages/Vital-Records.aspx>.

>   Domestic Violence Information

The PAJAR team cannot provide copies of restraining or protective orders. You can find Information about where and how to ask for help in domestic violence circumstances on the California Courts Self-Help website at selfhelp.courts.ca.gov/DV-restraining-order<https://selfhelp.courts.ca.gov/DV-restraining-order>.

If you want copies of a restraining or protective order, you must contact the superior court where the case is filed. You can find links to all 58 superior court websites at courts.ca.gov/find-my-court<https://courts.ca.gov/find-my-court>. Court websites list court telephone numbers and locations.

>   Criminal Background Records

The PAJAR team cannot provide copies of criminal history records. You must request copies from the superior court where the criminal case is filed. You can find links to all 58 superior court websites at courts.ca.gov/find-my-court<https://courts.ca.gov/find-my-court>. Court websites list court telephone numbers and locations.

Federal, state, and local law enforcement agencies are responsible for investigating criminal activity. If you suspect criminal activity is taking place, please contact law enforcement authorities. You can find a list of California Law Enforcement Agencies at post.ca.gov/le-agencies<https://post.ca.gov/le-agencies>.

>   Name Change Records

The PAJAR team cannot provide copies of name change records. The California State Archives stores some name change records. Please visit the State Archives website, at www.sos.ca.gov/archives/about-archives/contact<http://www.sos.ca.gov/archives/about-archives/contact/> to learn more. You can also call the California State Archives reference desk at 916-653-2246.

Information about the process for completing a name change can be found on the California Courts Self-Help website at selfhelp.courts.ca.gov/name-change<https://selfhelp.courts.ca.gov/name-change>.

>   Traffic Citations

The PAJAR team cannot provide copies of traffic citations. Each superior court handles traffic matters independently and is responsible for its own records. If you have questions about a traffic ticket, please contact the superior court listed on the ticket (i.e., the “Notice to Appear”). You can find links to all 58 superior court websites at courts.ca.gov/find-my-court<https://courts.ca.gov/find-my-court>. Court websites list court telephone numbers and locations.

You can find general information about traffic tickets on the California Courts Self-Help website at selfhelp.courts.ca.gov/traffic<https://selfhelp.courts.ca.gov/traffic>.

>   Wills

The PAJAR team cannot provide copies of a will for someone who has died. If a copy of the will has been filed or lodged with a superior court, you must contact the superior court to request a copy. You can find links to all 58 superior court websites at courts.ca.gov/find-my-court<https://courts.ca.gov/find-my-court>. Court websites list court telephone numbers and locations.

For general information about wills, estates, and probate matters, please visit the California Courts Self-Help website at selfhelp.courts.ca.gov/wills-estates-probate<https://selfhelp.courts.ca.gov/wills-estates-probate>.

>   Jury Duty Information

The PAJAR team cannot answer questions about jury duty. If you have questions about a juror summons, or want to be excused from jury duty, please contact the superior court that issued the jury summons. You can find links to all 58 superior court websites at courts.ca.gov/find-my-court<https://courts.ca.gov/find-my-court>. This page also contains links to the pages on each court’s website that has information about juror service. Court websites list court telephone numbers and locations.

You can find general information about jury service in California by visiting courts.ca.gov/courts/jury-service<https://courts.ca.gov/courts/jury-service>.

We hope this information is helpful. Thank you for your interest in the California judicial branch.

Sincerely,
Public Access to Judicial Administrative Records
Legal Services | Leadership Services Division
Judicial Council of California
455 Golden Gate Avenue
San Francisco, California 94102-3688
415-865-7796 | PAJAR@jud.ca.gov<mailto:PAJAR@jud.ca.gov> | courts.ca.gov/policy-administration/public-records<https://courts.ca.gov/policy-administration/public-records>

[cid:image002.png@01DC8BF2.164D3F60]

communications.subject: RE: California Public Records Act Request: California official reports RFP, agreements, correspondence (Supreme Court of California)
communications.communication:
Good morning,

Your below request has reached the “Public Access to Judicial Administrative Records” (PAJAR) team at the Judicial Council of California.  The PAJAR team responds to requests to inspect “judicial administrative records” pursuant to rule 10.500 of the California Rules of Court.  You can find information about rule 10.500, the process for requesting records, and the types of records available through this process at courts.ca.gov/policy-administration/public-records.

The PAJAR team is reviewing your request.  Properly processing your request will require additional time.  (Please see Cal. Rules of Court, rule 10.500(e)(8)(A) and (e)(8)(C).)  We estimate we will be able to notify you of our determination about whether the Judicial Council has disclosable responsive records by Thursday, March 5.  If our estimate changes, we will let you know without delay.

Sincerely,

Public Access to Judicial Administrative Records
Public Access Unit | Legal Services
Judicial Council of California
455 Golden Gate Avenue, San Francisco, California 94102-3688
415-865-7796 | PAJAR@jud.ca.gov<mailto:PAJAR@jud.ca.gov> | courts.ca.gov/policy-administration/public-records<https://courts.ca.gov/policy-administration/public-records>

Superior courts post their telephone numbers and street addresses on their websites. Some also post e-mail addresses.

Examples of records that you must request by contacting a superior court:

·         Divorce decrees;

·         Traffic citations;

·         Name change records;

·         Wills;

·         Criminal case records (for example, orders imposing sentences and judgments); and

·         Restraining orders.

You will find more information about some of these topics in the message below.

>   Court Case Records—Supreme Court of California and California Courts of Appeal

The PAJAR team cannot provide copies of appellate court case records. You can find information about specific cases filed with the Supreme Court of California or with the California Courts of Appeal at appellatecases.courtinfo.ca.gov<http://appellatecases.courtinfo.ca.gov/>.

You can review opinions that the Supreme Court of California and the California Courts of Appeal issued in specific cases at courts.ca.gov/opinions.htm<http://www.courts.ca.gov/opinions.htm>.

You can find the following Supreme Court of California case records online:

·         Legal briefs filed in cases argued before the court: supreme.courts.ca.gov/case-information/briefs-argued-cases<https://supreme.courts.ca.gov/case-information/briefs-argued-cases>; and

·         Official minutes going back three years: supreme.courts.ca.gov/case-information/minutes<https://supreme.courts.ca.gov/case-information/minutes>.

To view or request copies of records filed in a specific case before the Supreme Court of California, you must call or visit the court. Supreme Court contact information is posted at supreme.courts.ca.gov/about-court/court-locations-and-contacts<https://supreme.courts.ca.gov/about-court/court-locations-and-contacts>.

To view or request copies of records filed in specific cases before a California Court of Appeal you must call or visit the court. You can find a list of the six Courts of Appeal at appellate.courts.ca.gov<https://appellate.courts.ca.gov/>. On the left side of that page, select the court where the case is filed. Then scroll down to select “Contact Us.” On that page you will find court telephone numbers and location addresses.

If you have a Court of Appeal case number, but you do not know the court where the case is filed, please visit appellate.courts.ca.gov<https://appellate.courts.ca.gov/>. That page lists the letters that begin the case numbers for each of the Courts of Appeal.

Information about California appellate court cases also is available at your local law library, which may provide free public access to computer-assisted legal research systems such as LexisNexis or Westlaw. To learn more about local public law libraries, and to locate one near you, visit selfhelp.courts.ca.gov/court-basics/law-libraries<https://selfhelp.courts.ca.gov/court-basics/law-libraries>.

>   Court Case Records—United States Federal Courts


The PAJAR team cannot provide copies of records filed in cases before federal courts located in California. To find information about those federal courts—including court telephone numbers and location addresses for those courts—please visit:

  *   www.cand.uscourts.gov<http://www.cand.uscourts.gov> (district court, Northern California);
  *   www.casd.uscourts.gov<http://www.casd.uscourts.gov> (district court, Southern California);
  *   www.caed.uscourts.gov<http://www.caed.uscourts.gov> (district court, Eastern California);
  *   www.cacd.uscourts.gov<http://www.cacd.uscourts.gov> (district court, Central California); and
  *   www.ca9.uscourts.gov<http://www.ca9.uscourts.gov> (Courts of Appeal).

>   Birth or Death Certificates

The PAJAR team cannot provide copies of birth or death certificates. The registrar or recorder in the county where the birth or death occurred would have these records.

You can find a list of all California county registrars and recorders, and their contact information, at www.cdph.ca.gov/Programs/CHSI/Pages/County-Registrars-and-Recorders.aspx<http://www.cdph.ca.gov/Programs/CHSI/Pages/County-Registrars-and-Recorders.aspx>.

For information about how to request a birth or death certificate, please visit the website of California’s Department of Public Health, at www.cdph.ca.gov/Programs/CHSI/Pages/Vital-Records.aspx<http://www.cdph.ca.gov/Programs/CHSI/Pages/Vital-Records.aspx>.

>   Domestic Violence Information

The PAJAR team cannot provide copies of restraining or protective orders. You can find Information about where and how to ask for help in domestic violence circumstances on the California Courts Self-Help website at selfhelp.courts.ca.gov/DV-restraining-order<https://selfhelp.courts.ca.gov/DV-restraining-order>.

If you want copies of a restraining or protective order, you must contact the superior court where the case is filed. You can find links to all 58 superior court websites at courts.ca.gov/find-my-court<https://courts.ca.gov/find-my-court>. Court websites list court telephone numbers and locations.

>   Criminal Background Records

The PAJAR team cannot provide copies of criminal history records. You must request copies from the superior court where the criminal case is filed. You can find links to all 58 superior court websites at courts.ca.gov/find-my-court<https://courts.ca.gov/find-my-court>. Court websites list court telephone numbers and locations.

Federal, state, and local law enforcement agencies are responsible for investigating criminal activity. If you suspect criminal activity is taking place, please contact law enforcement authorities. You can find a list of California Law Enforcement Agencies at post.ca.gov/le-agencies<https://post.ca.gov/le-agencies>.

>   Name Change Records

The PAJAR team cannot provide copies of name change records. The California State Archives stores some name change records. Please visit the State Archives website, at www.sos.ca.gov/archives/about-archives/contact<http://www.sos.ca.gov/archives/about-archives/contact/> to learn more. You can also call the California State Archives reference desk at 916-653-2246.

Information about the process for completing a name change can be found on the California Courts Self-Help website at selfhelp.courts.ca.gov/name-change<https://selfhelp.courts.ca.gov/name-change>.

>   Traffic Citations

The PAJAR team cannot provide copies of traffic citations. Each superior court handles traffic matters independently and is responsible for its own records. If you have questions about a traffic ticket, please contact the superior court listed on the ticket (i.e., the “Notice to Appear”). You can find links to all 58 superior court websites at courts.ca.gov/find-my-court<https://courts.ca.gov/find-my-court>. Court websites list court telephone numbers and locations.

You can find general information about traffic tickets on the California Courts Self-Help website at selfhelp.courts.ca.gov/traffic<https://selfhelp.courts.ca.gov/traffic>.

>   Wills

The PAJAR team cannot provide copies of a will for someone who has died. If a copy of the will has been filed or lodged with a superior court, you must contact the superior court to request a copy. You can find links to all 58 superior court websites at courts.ca.gov/find-my-court<https://courts.ca.gov/find-my-court>. Court websites list court telephone numbers and locations.

For general information about wills, estates, and probate matters, please visit the California Courts Self-Help website at selfhelp.courts.ca.gov/wills-estates-probate<https://selfhelp.courts.ca.gov/wills-estates-probate>.

>   Jury Duty Information

The PAJAR team cannot answer questions about jury duty. If you have questions about a juror summons, or want to be excused from jury duty, please contact the superior court that issued the jury summons. You can find links to all 58 superior court websites at courts.ca.gov/find-my-court<https://courts.ca.gov/find-my-court>. This page also contains links to the pages on each court’s website that has information about juror service. Court websites list court telephone numbers and locations.

You can find general information about jury service in California by visiting courts.ca.gov/courts/jury-service<https://courts.ca.gov/courts/jury-service>.

We hope this information is helpful. Thank you for your interest in the California judicial branch.

Sincerely,
Public Access to Judicial Administrative Records
Legal Services | Leadership Services Division
Judicial Council of California
455 Golden Gate Avenue
San Francisco, California 94102-3688
415-865-7796 | PAJAR@jud.ca.gov<mailto:PAJAR@jud.ca.gov> | courts.ca.gov/policy-administration/public-records<https://courts.ca.gov/policy-administration/public-records>

communications.communication:
Hello,

We are responding to your March 3 email, below, seeking a status update on your records request that we received on February 9, also below.

On February 17, we acknowledged your records request and estimated that, by March 5, we would inform you of our determination of whether there are disclosable records responsive to your request.  (See Cal. Rules of Court, rule 10.500(e)(6), (e)(8).)  Our February 17 email is included in the below chain.  We did not estimate that your records request would be processed and completed by March 5.

We have determined that the Supreme Court of California has disclosable responsive records.  We estimate we will be able to provide you with copies by Thursday, March 26.  If we determine we need to change this estimate, we will notify you without delay.

Sincerely,

455 Golden Gate Avenue, San Francisco, California 94102-3688

Good morning,

Your below request has reached the “Public Access to Judicial Administrative Records” (PAJAR) team at the Judicial Council of California. The PAJAR team responds to requests to inspect “judicial administrative records” pursuant to rule 10.500 of the California Rules of Court. You can find information about rule 10.500, the process for requesting records, and the types of records available through this process at courts.ca.gov/policy-administration/public-records.

*** PLEASE DO NOT REPLY to this e-mail message. Responses to this automatic reply are not monitored.***

WHAT TO EXPECT

* If you are asking for court case records—for example, a court order, divorce records, name change records, criminal history records— this message will be the only response you receive. Please read the section below, The PAJAR Team Cannot Provide These Records, to find information that may be helpful.

· Divorce decrees;

Then scroll down to select “Contact Us.” On that page you will find court telephone numbers and location addresses.

On Feb. 8, 2026:
Subject: California Public Records Act Request: California official reports RFP, agreements, correspondence (Supreme Court of California)
To Whom It May Concern:

Pursuant to the California Public Records Act, I hereby request the following records:

The below requests are all in connection with the California Supreme Court's Request for Proposal RFP-CAL-2023-01-RP for publication of the California official reports (the "RFP"), made under Government Code section 68903, and which is available at https://courts.ca.gov/request/rfp-cal-2023-01-rp/rfp/publication-california-official-reports.

Government Code section 68903 states:

"The official reports shall be published under a contract to be entered into on behalf of the state by the Chief Justice of California, the Secretary of State, the Attorney General, the President of the State Bar, and the Reporter of Decisions, who shall serve as secretary."

REQUESTS:

1. All signed agreements made under proposals received pursuant to the RFP, including subsequent signed amendments (collectively, the "Agreements").
2. All correspondence with LexisNexis about the RFP.
3. All correspondence with LexisNexis about the Agreements.
4. All proposals received in response to the RFP.
5. All internal correspondence related to the RFP.

If assessing any confidentiality concerns, please note the following language in the RFP:

11.0 CONFIDENTIAL OR PROPRIETARY INFORMATION
PROPOSALS ARE SUBJECT TO DISCLOSURE TO THIRD PARTIES AND MEMBERS OF THE PUBLIC PURSUANT TO APPLICABLE LAWS, INCLUDING PUBLIC DISCLOSURE PURSUANT TO RULE 10.500 OF THE CALIFORNIA RULES OF COURT. Except as required by law, neither the Judicial Council nor the Court will disclose (i) social security numbers, or (ii) balance sheets or income statements submitted by a Publisher that is not a publicly traded corporation. All other information in Proposals may be disclosed in response to applicable public records requests, or as otherwise required by law. Such disclosure may be made regardless of whether the Proposal (or portions thereof) is marked “confidential,” “proprietary,” “copyright ©,” or otherwise, and regardless of any statement in the Proposal (a) purporting to limit the Judicial Council’s or Court’s right to disclose information in the Proposal, or (b) requiring the Judicial Council or Court to inform or obtain the consent of the Publisher prior to the disclosure of the Proposal (or portions thereof). Any Proposal that is password protected, or contains portions that are password protected, may be rejected. Submission of any Proposal pursuant to this RFP constitutes acknowledgment and consent by the Publisher to the potential public disclosure of its Proposal content, as set forth in this section. Publishers are accordingly cautioned not to include confidential, proprietary, or privileged information in proposals.

The requested documents will be made available to the general public, and this request is not being made for commercial purposes.

In the event that there are fees, I would be grateful if you would inform me of the total charges in advance of fulfilling my request. I would prefer the request filled electronically, by e-mail attachment if available, or by USB drive, CD-ROM, or online file sharing system if not.

Thank you in advance for your anticipated cooperation in this matter. I look forward to receiving your response to this request within 10 calendar days, as the statute requires.

Sincerely,

Ansel Halliburton

View request history, upload responsive documents, and report problems here:
https://www.muckrock.com/respond/2232448/

Filed via MuckRock.com
E-mail (Preferred): 204384-12675133@requests.muckrock.com<mailto:204384-12675133@requests.muckrock.com>

PLEASE NOTE OUR NEW ADDRESS
For mailed responses, please address (see note):
MuckRock News
DEPT MR 204384
263 Huntington Ave
Boston, MA 02115

PLEASE NOTE: This request is not filed by a MuckRock staff member, but is being sent through MuckRock by the above in order to better track, share, and manage public records requests. Also note that improperly addressed (i.e., with the requester's name rather than "MuckRock News" and the department number) requests might be returned as undeliverable.

communications.communication:
Good afternoon,

We are following up on your requests for records related to the California Supreme Court's Request for Proposal RFP-CAL-2023-01-RP<https://courts.ca.gov/request/rfp-cal-2023-01-rp/rfp/publication-california-official-reports>.  On February 9, 2026, you submitted two seemingly identical records requests and we did not immediately note they were addressed to different recipients, i.e., the Judicial Council of California and the California Supreme Court.  Apologies for the oversight.

Both the California Supreme Court and the Judicial Council have disclosable records.  Our previous estimate that we would provide you disclosable records by Thursday, March 26, remains the same.  If we determine we need to change this estimate, we will notify you without delay.

Going forward, we will send any correspondence regarding these two requests to the above email address (204383-29715987@requests.muckrock.com<mailto:204383-29715987@requests.muckrock.com>), copying the second email address (204384-12675133@requests.muckrock.com<mailto:204384-12675133@requests.muckrock.com>).

Sincerely,

Public Access to Judicial Administrative Records
Public Access Unit | Legal Services
Judicial Council of California
455 Golden Gate Avenue, San Francisco, California 94102-3688
415-865-7796 | PAJAR@jud.ca.gov<mailto:PAJAR@jud.ca.gov> | courts.ca.gov/policy-administration/public-records<https://courts.ca.gov/policy-administration/public-records>

From: PAJAR <PAJAR@jud.ca.gov>

On Feb. 8, 2026:
Subject: Thank you for your request. (Autoreply)
Thank you for contacting the Judicial Council of California's Public Access to Judicial Administrative Records (PAJAR) team. This message is an automatic reply to let you know that we received your request.

Please read the message below because it has information that may help you.

*** PLEASE DO NOT REPLY to this e-mail message. Responses to this automatic reply are not monitored.***

WHAT TO EXPECT

* If you are asking for court case records-for example, a court order, divorce records, name change records, criminal history records- this message will be the only response you receive. Please read the section below, The PAJAR Team Cannot Provide These Records, to find information that may be helpful.

* If you are asking for judicial administrative records from the Judicial Council, the Supreme Court of California, or a California Court of Appeal, the PAJAR team will send you a response within about 10 days.

* Here are some examples of judicial administrative records: signed contracts, final audit reports, budget and spending reports, reports to the Legislature, and procurement records. You can find more information about judicial administrative records at courts.ca.gov/cms/rules/index/ten/rule10_500<https://courts.ca.gov/cms/rules/index/ten/rule10_500> and at courts.ca.gov/policy-administration/public-records<https://courts.ca.gov/policy-administration/public-records>.

* The PAJAR team may ask you for more information to be sure it understands your request. The PAJAR team may also notify you if it needs more time to complete a search.

* When work on your request is complete, the PAJAR team will notify you whether there are disclosable responsive records or not. If there are disclosable responsive copies, the PAJAR team will provide electronic copies, if possible.

THE PAJAR TEAM CANNOT PROVIDE THESE RECORDS

> Court Case Records-Superior Courts of California

The PAJAR team cannot provide copies of superior court case records. If you want records filed in a specific superior court case, you must contact the court directly. California has 58 superior courts, one in each county. You can find links to each court's website at courts.ca.gov/find-my-court<https://courts.ca.gov/find-my-court>. Each superior court clerk is responsible for that court's records.

Some superior court websites allow the public to search and view some types of case records online. If a court's website does not allow you to search and view case records online, you must call or visit the court clerk's office for help. Superior courts post their telephone numbers and street addresses on their websites. Some also post e-mail addresses.

Examples of records that you must request by contacting a superior court:

* Divorce decrees;

* Traffic citations;

* Name change records;

* Wills;

* Criminal case records (for example, orders imposing sentences and judgments); and

* Restraining orders.

You will find more information about some of these topics in the message below.

> Court Case Records-Supreme Court of California and California Courts of Appeal

The PAJAR team cannot provide copies of appellate court case records. You can find information about specific cases filed with the Supreme Court of California or with the California Courts of Appeal at appellatecases.courtinfo.ca.gov<http://appellatecases.courtinfo.ca.gov/>.

You can review opinions that the Supreme Court of California and the California Courts of Appeal issued in specific cases at courts.ca.gov/opinions.htm<http://www.courts.ca.gov/opinions.htm>.

You can find the following Supreme Court of California case records online:

* Legal briefs filed in cases argued before the court: supreme.courts.ca.gov/case-information/briefs-argued-cases<https://supreme.courts.ca.gov/case-information/briefs-argued-cases>; and

* Official minutes going back three years: supreme.courts.ca.gov/case-information/minutes<https://supreme.courts.ca.gov/case-information/minutes>.

To view or request copies of records filed in a specific case before the Supreme Court of California, you must call or visit the court. Supreme Court contact information is posted at supreme.courts.ca.gov/about-court/court-locations-and-contacts<https://supreme.courts.ca.gov/about-court/court-locations-and-contacts>.

To view or request copies of records filed in specific cases before a California Court of Appeal you must call or visit the court. You can find a list of the six Courts of Appeal at appellate.courts.ca.gov<https://appellate.courts.ca.gov/>. On the left side of that page, select the court where the case is filed. Then scroll down to select "Contact Us." On that page you will find court telephone numbers and location addresses.

If you have a Court of Appeal case number, but you do not know the court where the case is filed, please visit appellate.courts.ca.gov<https://appellate.courts.ca.gov/>. That page lists the letters that begin the case numbers for each of the Courts of Appeal.

Information about California appellate court cases also is available at your local law library, which may provide free public access to computer-assisted legal research systems such as LexisNexis or Westlaw. To learn more about local public law libraries, and to locate one near you, visit selfhelp.courts.ca.gov/court-basics/law-libraries<https://selfhelp.courts.ca.gov/court-basics/law-libraries>.

> Court Case Records-United States Federal Courts

The PAJAR team cannot provide copies of records filed in cases before federal courts located in California. To find information about those federal courts-including court telephone numbers and location addresses for those courts-please visit:

* www.cand.uscourts.gov<http://www.cand.uscourts.gov> (district court, Northern California);
* www.casd.uscourts.gov<http://www.casd.uscourts.gov> (district court, Southern California);
* www.caed.uscourts.gov<http://www.caed.uscourts.gov> (district court, Eastern California);
* www.cacd.uscourts.gov<http://www.cacd.uscourts.gov> (district court, Central California); and
* www.ca9.uscourts.gov<http://www.ca9.uscourts.gov> (Courts of Appeal).

> Birth or Death Certificates

The PAJAR team cannot provide copies of birth or death certificates. The registrar or recorder in the county where the birth or death occurred would have these records.

You can find a list of all California county registrars and recorders, and their contact information, at www.cdph.ca.gov/Programs/CHSI/Pages/County-Registrars-and-Recorders.aspx<http://www.cdph.ca.gov/Programs/CHSI/Pages/County-Registrars-and-Recorders.aspx<http://www.cdph.ca.gov/Programs/CHSI/Pages/County-Registrars-and-Recorders.aspx%3chttp:/www.cdph.ca.gov/Programs/CHSI/Pages/County-Registrars-and-Recorders.aspx<http://www.cdph.ca.gov/Programs/CHSI/Pages/County-Registrars-and-Recorders.aspx%3chttp:/www.cdph.ca.gov/Programs/CHSI/Pages/County-Registrars-and-Recorders.aspx%3chttp:/www.cdph.ca.gov/Programs/CHSI/Pages/County-Registrars-and-Recorders.aspx%3chttp:/www.cdph.ca.gov/Programs/CHSI/Pages/County-Registrars-and-Recorders.aspx>>>.

For information about how to request a birth or death certificate, please visit the website of California's Department of Public Health, at www.cdph.ca.gov/Programs/CHSI/Pages/Vital-Records.aspx<http://www.cdph.ca.gov/Programs/CHSI/Pages/Vital-Records.aspx<http://www.cdph.ca.gov/Programs/CHSI/Pages/Vital-Records.aspx%3chttp:/www.cdph.ca.gov/Programs/CHSI/Pages/Vital-Records.aspx<http://www.cdph.ca.gov/Programs/CHSI/Pages/Vital-Records.aspx%3chttp:/www.cdph.ca.gov/Programs/CHSI/Pages/Vital-Records.aspx%3chttp:/www.cdph.ca.gov/Programs/CHSI/Pages/Vital-Records.aspx%3chttp:/www.cdph.ca.gov/Programs/CHSI/Pages/Vital-Records.aspx>>>.

> Domestic Violence Information

The PAJAR team cannot provide copies of restraining or protective orders. You can find Information about where and how to ask for help in domestic violence circumstances on the California Courts Self-Help website at selfhelp.courts.ca.gov/DV-restraining-order<https://selfhelp.courts.ca.gov/DV-restraining-order>.

If you want copies of a restraining or protective order, you must contact the superior court where the case is filed. You can find links to all 58 superior court websites at courts.ca.gov/find-my-court<https://courts.ca.gov/find-my-court>. Court websites list court telephone numbers and locations.

> Criminal Background Records

The PAJAR team cannot provide copies of criminal history records. You must request copies from the superior court where the criminal case is filed. You can find links to all 58 superior court websites at courts.ca.gov/find-my-court<https://courts.ca.gov/find-my-court>. Court websites list court telephone numbers and locations.

Federal, state, and local law enforcement agencies are responsible for investigating criminal activity. If you suspect criminal activity is taking place, please contact law enforcement authorities. You can find a list of California Law Enforcement Agencies at post.ca.gov/le-agencies<https://post.ca.gov/le-agencies>.

> Name Change Records

The PAJAR team cannot provide copies of name change records. The California State Archives stores some name change records. Please visit the State Archives website, at www.sos.ca.gov/archives/about-archives/contact<http://www.sos.ca.gov/archives/about-archives/contact/<http://www.sos.ca.gov/archives/about-archives/contact%3chttp:/www.sos.ca.gov/archives/about-archives/contact/<http://www.sos.ca.gov/archives/about-archives/contact%3chttp:/www.sos.ca.gov/archives/about-archives/contact/%3chttp:/www.sos.ca.gov/archives/about-archives/contact%3chttp:/www.sos.ca.gov/archives/about-archives/contact/>>> to learn more. You can also call the California State Archives reference desk at 916-653-2246.

Information about the process for completing a name change can be found on the California Courts Self-Help website at selfhelp.courts.ca.gov/name-change<https://selfhelp.courts.ca.gov/name-change>.

> Traffic Citations

The PAJAR team cannot provide copies of traffic citations. Each superior court handles traffic matters independently and is responsible for its own records. If you have questions about a traffic ticket, please contact the superior court listed on the ticket (i.e., the "Notice to Appear"). You can find links to all 58 superior court websites at courts.ca.gov/find-my-court<https://courts.ca.gov/find-my-court>. Court websites list court telephone numbers and locations.

You can find general information about traffic tickets on the California Courts Self-Help website at selfhelp.courts.ca.gov/traffic<https://selfhelp.courts.ca.gov/traffic>.

> Wills

The PAJAR team cannot provide copies of a will for someone who has died. If a copy of the will has been filed or lodged with a superior court, you must contact the superior court to request a copy. You can find links to all 58 superior court websites at courts.ca.gov/find-my-court<https://courts.ca.gov/find-my-court>. Court websites list court telephone numbers and locations.

For general information about wills, estates, and probate matters, please visit the California Courts Self-Help website at selfhelp.courts.ca.gov/wills-estates-probate<https://selfhelp.courts.ca.gov/wills-estates-probate>.

> Jury Duty Information

The PAJAR team cannot answer questions about jury duty. If you have questions about a juror summons, or want to be excused from jury duty, please contact the superior court that issued the jury summons. You can find links to all 58 superior court websites at courts.ca.gov/find-my-court<https://courts.ca.gov/find-my-court>. This page also contains links to the pages on each court's website that has information about juror service. Court websites list court telephone numbers and locations.

You can find general information about jury service in California by visiting courts.ca.gov/courts/jury-service<https://courts.ca.gov/courts/jury-service>.

We hope this information is helpful. Thank you for your interest in the California judicial branch.

Sincerely,
Public Access to Judicial Administrative Records
Legal Services | Leadership Services Division
Judicial Council of California
455 Golden Gate Avenue
San Francisco, California 94102-3688

On Feb. 8, 2026:
Subject: California Public Records Act Request: California official reports RFP, agreements, correspondence (Judicial Council of California)
To Whom It May Concern:

Pursuant to the California Public Records Act, I hereby request the following records:

The below requests are all in connection with the California Supreme Court's Request for Proposal RFP-CAL-2023-01-RP for publication of the California official reports (the "RFP"), made under Government Code section 68903, and which is available at https://courts.ca.gov/request/rfp-cal-2023-01-rp/rfp/publication-california-official-reports.

Government Code section 68903 states:

"The official reports shall be published under a contract to be entered into on behalf of the state by the Chief Justice of California, the Secretary of State, the Attorney General, the President of the State Bar, and the Reporter of Decisions, who shall serve as secretary."

REQUESTS:

1. All signed agreements made under proposals received pursuant to the RFP, including subsequent signed amendments (collectively, the "Agreements").
2. All correspondence with LexisNexis about the RFP.
3. All correspondence with LexisNexis about the Agreements.
4. All proposals received in response to the RFP.
5. All internal correspondence related to the RFP.

If assessing any confidentiality concerns, please note the following language in the RFP:

11.0 CONFIDENTIAL OR PROPRIETARY INFORMATION
PROPOSALS ARE SUBJECT TO DISCLOSURE TO THIRD PARTIES AND MEMBERS OF THE PUBLIC PURSUANT TO APPLICABLE LAWS, INCLUDING PUBLIC DISCLOSURE PURSUANT TO RULE 10.500 OF THE CALIFORNIA RULES OF COURT. Except as required by law, neither the Judicial Council nor the Court will disclose (i) social security numbers, or (ii) balance sheets or income statements submitted by a Publisher that is not a publicly traded corporation. All other information in Proposals may be disclosed in response to applicable public records requests, or as otherwise required by law. Such disclosure may be made regardless of whether the Proposal (or portions thereof) is marked "confidential," "proprietary," "copyright (c)," or otherwise, and regardless of any statement in the Proposal (a) purporting to limit the Judicial Council's or Court's right to disclose information in the Proposal, or (b) requiring the Judicial Council or Court to inform or obtain the consent of the Publisher prior to the disclosure of the Proposal (or portions thereof). Any Proposal that is password protected, or contains portions that are password protected, may be rejected. Submission of any Proposal pursuant to this RFP constitutes acknowledgment and consent by the Publisher to the potential public disclosure of its Proposal content, as set forth in this section. Publishers are accordingly cautioned not to include confidential, proprietary, or privileged information in proposals.

The requested documents will be made available to the general public, and this request is not being made for commercial purposes.

In the event that there are fees, I would be grateful if you would inform me of the total charges in advance of fulfilling my request. I would prefer the request filled electronically, by e-mail attachment if available, or by USB drive, CD-ROM, or online file sharing system if not.

Thank you in advance for your anticipated cooperation in this matter. I look forward to receiving your response to this request within 10 calendar days, as the statute requires.

Sincerely,

Ansel Halliburton

The PAJAR team cannot provide copies of criminal history records. You must request copies from the superior court where the criminal case is filed. You can find links to all 58 superior court websites at courts.ca.gov/find-my-court<https://courts.ca.gov/find-my-court>. Court websites list court telephone numbers and locations.

Federal, state, and local law enforcement agencies are responsible for investigating criminal activity. If you suspect criminal activity is taking place, please contact law enforcement authorities. You can find a list of California Law Enforcement Agencies at post.ca.gov/le-agencies<https://post.ca.gov/le-agencies>.

> Name Change Records

The PAJAR team cannot provide copies of name change records. The California State Archives stores some name change records. Please visit the State Archives website, at www.sos.ca.gov/archives/about-archives/contact<http://www.sos.ca.gov/archives/about-archives/contact/<http://www.sos.ca.gov/archives/about-archives/contact%3chttp:/www.sos.ca.gov/archives/about-archives/contact/>> to learn more. You can also call the California State Archives reference desk at 916-653-2246.

Information about the process for completing a name change can be found on the California Courts Self-Help website at selfhelp.courts.ca.gov/name-change<https://selfhelp.courts.ca.gov/name-change>.

> Traffic Citations

The PAJAR team cannot provide copies of traffic citations. Each superior court handles traffic matters independently and is responsible for its own records. If you have questions about a traffic ticket, please contact the superior court listed on the ticket (i.e., the "Notice to Appear"). You can find links to all 58 superior court websites at courts.ca.gov/find-my-court<https://courts.ca.gov/find-my-court>. Court websites list court telephone numbers and locations.

You can find general information about traffic tickets on the California Courts Self-Help website at selfhelp.courts.ca.gov/traffic<https://selfhelp.courts.ca.gov/traffic>.

> Wills

The PAJAR team cannot provide copies of a will for someone who has died. If a copy of the will has been filed or lodged with a superior court, you must contact the superior court to request a copy. You can find links to all 58 superior court websites at courts.ca.gov/find-my-court<https://courts.ca.gov/find-my-court>. Court websites list court telephone numbers and locations.

For general information about wills, estates, and probate matters, please visit the California Courts Self-Help website at selfhelp.courts.ca.gov/wills-estates-probate<https://selfhelp.courts.ca.gov/wills-estates-probate>.

> Jury Duty Information

The PAJAR team cannot answer questions about jury duty. If you have questions about a juror summons, or want to be excused from jury duty, please contact the superior court that issued the jury summons. You can find links to all 58 superior court websites at courts.ca.gov/find-my-court<https://courts.ca.gov/find-my-court>. This page also contains links to the pages on each court's website that has information about juror service. Court websites list court telephone numbers and locations.

You can find general information about jury service in California by visiting courts.ca.gov/courts/jury-service<https://courts.ca.gov/courts/jury-service>.

We hope this information is helpful. Thank you for your interest in the California judicial branch.

Sincerely,
